title: Dog Laceration on Ear Flap: First Aid and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out what to do when your dog's ear flap is bleeding. Learn about first aid, urgency levels, and why triage photos are essential for care.
content_html:
What is an Ear Flap Laceration?

An ear flap laceration is a cut, tear, or puncture to the pinna, which is the outer part of your dog's ear. This area is heavily supplied with blood vessels, which is why even a relatively small wound can result in significant, steady bleeding. When a dog shakes its head due to the discomfort or the sensation of blood, it often causes the wound to reopen or splatter, making the situation look quite dramatic.

Is it an Emergency?

This condition is generally classified as a Medium Urgency situation. While a lacerated ear is rarely life-threatening, the bleeding can be difficult to manage at home and the wound is highly susceptible to infection or the formation of an aural hematoma. You should seek veterinary care promptly if the bleeding does not stop with pressure, if the cut is deep, or if it extends through the cartilage of the ear.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear photo of the injury to a veterinary professional can significantly speed up the triage process. A photo allows a vet to assess the severity, location, and type of wound without immediate physical contact, which can be stressful for a dog in pain. It helps the clinical team determine if your dog needs immediate surgical repair (stitches) or if the wound can be managed with conservative wound care and bandaging.

faqs.question: How can I stop the bleeding right now?
faqs.answer: Apply firm, continuous pressure to the site of the bleeding using a clean towel or gauze for at least 10 minutes without stopping to check if it has stopped.

faqs.question: Should I let my dog shake their head?
faqs.answer: No. Head shaking will prevent clotting and cause the wound to bleed more. Try to keep your dog calm or gently wrap the ear against the head using a soft bandage until you reach a vet.

faqs.question: Does my dog need stitches for an ear cut?
faqs.answer: Many ear lacerations do require stitches because the skin on the ear is thin and the constant movement of the ear flap makes it difficult for the wound to stay closed on its own.
